title: PODCAST | The Spillover: What Will the Fed Become Under Chair Kevin Warsh? | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Former Fed Vice Chair Roger Ferguson argues the Warsh-led Fed is more likely to hike than ease, with core inflation stuck above 3%, unresolved energy pressures from the Iran war, and AI driving capex demand that pushes market rates higher. Warsh has launched five task forces (communication, inflation mandate, productivity/jobs, data collection, balance-sheet framework) that should move the Fed forward but unevenly and slower than hoped.

insights.claim: Current U.S. economic conditions point toward a Fed rate hike rather than an ease, with markets now pricing in the possibility of one hike this year and possibly one next year.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Positions against the market's prior easing bias; favors short-duration / front-end rate exposure and caution on rate-sensitive longs.
insights.quote: I would definitely be in the camp of thinking that it was probably, you know, time to hike.
insights.locator: I've written and said a few places

insights.claim: U.S. core inflation appears stuck above three percent and is not quickly returning to the Fed's two percent target, which has now been missed for five years.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Sticky core inflation undercuts rate-cut expectations and supports a higher-for-longer or hiking rate path.
insights.quote: core inflation now is stuck somewhere over three, not quickly returning to two
insights.locator: I've written and said a few places

insights.claim: Chair Warsh struck a hawkish tone and made the Fed's commitment to returning to two percent inflation unanimous and unambiguous, raising the risk the Fed must act to back the bold statement.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: A hawkish, commitment-bound chair raises the bar for cuts and the odds of earlier-than-expected tightening.
insights.quote: because chair Warsh sounded, to use the word, hawkish, some talk of hikes earlier rather than later
insights.locator: I've written and said a few places

insights.claim: Reaching the two percent inflation target may be harder than expected for structural reasons, and the Fed may come to regret its bold unanimous commitment to that target.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Signals persistent inflation risk premium; watch for structural inflation themes (energy, labor, capex) that keep rates elevated.
insights.locator: I've written and said a few places

insights.claim: AI is currently acting as an inflationary rather than disinflationary force by driving a surge in capital expenditures, which calls for more savings and implies market interest rates should be somewhat higher to equilibrate increased demand for capital.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI-disinflation consensus; supports higher real rates and a structural bid under bond yields, relevant for duration positioning.
insights.quote: what AI is driving mainly is an increase in capital expenditures, calling for, you know, more savings to go out. And that means to me market rate should be somewhat higher
insights.locator: The other thing that you put your finger on

insights.claim: A major technology provider has signaled it will raise consumer-goods prices because surging AI-driven input demand is pushing up its costs, showing AI is not yet delivering disinflation.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Real-world evidence of AI-driven cost-push inflation; watch tech and hardware input pricing as an inflation signal.
insights.quote: one of the biggest technology providers announcing that they're probably going to be raising prices for some of their consumer goods

insights.claim: China's universities have killed or suspended over 12,000 undergraduate degree programs and introduced 10,000 new ones in four years—about 30% of all programs—to steer the labor force toward AI-era jobs via top-down control.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Illustrates divergent state-directed vs. market AI labor adaptation; relevant for China growth/productivity and education-policy theses.
insights.quote: China's universities have killed or suspended more than 12,000 undergraduate degrees in the last four years. They've introduced 10,000 new degree programs over the same period.

insights.claim: AI adoption will pit China's top-down state-directed labor model against the messier U.S. market-driven model, and Ferguson is uncertain the Chinese central-planning approach will work.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Macro framing for AI-era competitiveness; watch which system better redeploys displaced workers and generates productive new jobs.
insights.quote: this world of AI is going to be a collision of two views of how one runs an economy
insights.locator: No, look, I agree with you

themes.theme: Fed structural reform and task forces

insights.claim: Warsh has created five Fed task forces covering communication, the inflation mandate, productivity and jobs, data collection, and a balance-sheet framework, with some outside experts and a possible year-end timeline for proposals.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: These task forces are the roadmap for structural Fed change; watch outputs for shifts in communication, dot plot, and balance-sheet policy.

insights.claim: Well-led Fed task forces historically move the institution forward, but progress will be uneven and slower than desired, with the year-end deadline possible but not assured.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Tempers expectations for rapid structural change; investors should not front-run task-force outcomes on a tight timeline.
insights.quote: This is the kind of thing where you want to, you know, measure twice, cut once.
insights.locator: First, thank you for the kind words

insights.claim: The dot plot is likely to be revised or done away with under Warsh, who declined to submit his own dot while encouraging colleagues to, viewing it as 19 confusing conditional statements rather than a forecast.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: A changed or removed dot plot alters how markets read Fed guidance; watch for reduced forward-rate signaling from the SEP.
insights.quote: that strikes me as one that's probably going to be revised and maybe, you know, done away with

insights.claim: The Fed never established a clear framework for when and how quickly to use or unwind its balance sheet as a monetary or financial-stability tool, and Warsh is expected to build one.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: A formal QE/QT framework would reduce liquidity-policy uncertainty; watch for balance-sheet rules affecting bond market liquidity.
insights.quote: I would expect a framework around when and how they use a balance sheet as a financial stability tool.
insights.locator: That's, I think, in this listing

themes.theme: Communication, forward guidance and market spillovers

insights.claim: Fed forward guidance has always been conditional, but market participants run with it as a foregone conclusion, creating miscommunication and volatility when expected actions do not materialize.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Explains episodic rate-market dislocations; traders should treat Fed guidance as conditional reaction functions, not commitments.
insights.quote: the market participants, you know, running with the forward guidance as though it were a foregone conclusion, not conditional

insights.claim: The Fed's single national interest rate cannot target individual sectors like housing or specific prices, and longer-term Treasury supply-demand dynamics matter more than the fed funds rate for mortgage rates and housing.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Don't expect Fed cuts to fix housing; watch the long end of the curve and Treasury issuance for mortgage and housing signals.
insights.locator: I could keep going down our fed operations

insights.claim: The Fed's goal is controlling inflation, not supporting housing, specific sectors, or equity prices, contradicting the notion of a 'fed put' under equities.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Challenges reliance on a fed put; equity investors should not assume the Fed will backstop markets via policy.
insights.quote: Frankly, it's not to support, contrary what people think, equity prices. Some people think there's a so-called fed put.
insights.locator: Yeah, but at the end of the day

insights.claim: Energy-driven inflation pressure from the Iran war remains unresolved because infrastructure destruction has already occurred and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz may come with tolls and tariffs affecting price levels.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Keeps an energy inflation tail risk live even if the conflict de-escalates; watch oil prices and Hormuz transit terms.
insights.quote: the need to, you know, reopen the strait. It's not clear if the Strait of Hormuz is going to be open, freer passage, or with, you know, various kinds of tolls and tariffs
insights.locator: I've written and said a few places

insights.claim: Fed dollar swap lines are critical during moments of global dollar-liquidity stress that spill over to the rest of the world, and Warsh has floated a new Fed-Treasury accord while keeping monetary-policy independence intact.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Swap-line availability and a Fed-Treasury accord shape global dollar liquidity and FX; watch for accord details affecting the dollar.
insights.locator: Broadly speaking, I think it's critical
